html:
MarketEnterprise Co., Ltd. (TSE: 3135, ¥1,199)

MarketEnterprise is not the net-cash, 3x EBIT bargain described in the prior draft. The correct setup is more interesting and more fragile: a tiny TSE Prime recommerce and WiMAX subscriber platform whose FY2026 earnings were crushed by a fixable mobile customer-acquisition mistake, while the core reuse business is quietly accelerating.

At ¥1,199, the market cap is about ¥6.4B. With ¥1.31B cash, roughly ¥2.05B interest-bearing liabilities, and ¥0.47B noncontrolling interest, enterprise value is roughly ¥7.6B. On the company’s current FY6/2026 guide of ¥26.9B revenue, ¥180M operating profit, and only ¥10M parent net income, the stock screens terribly: about 640x guided earnings. That is exactly why the opportunity exists. The current multiple is not cheap on depressed FY2026 guidance. It is cheap if FY2026 is a trough in mobile profitability and the segment data are telling the truth.

The business has two engines. The first is internet-based reuse: “Takakuureru.com,” CtoBtoC purchase and resale, used farm machinery, used autos, and “Oikura,” a matching platform that connects households with reuse shops. This is not a pure Mercari competitor. The company focuses on categories where consumers do not want to price, pack, authenticate, or ship items themselves, especially large, high-value, or multi-item disposal situations. It buys through home visits, courier, and stores, then resells through Yahoo! Auctions, Rakuten, Mercari, Amazon, and its own channels. The second engine is ME Mobile, mainly Kashimo WiMAX, where revenue includes stock-like monthly line usage fees plus shot-type sales incentives tied to new subscriber acquisition.

The variant perception is that investors are looking at the consolidated FY2026 collapse and missing the split. In the May 14, 2026 Q3 results, nine-month revenue rose 9.5% to ¥19.45B, but operating profit fell to ¥78M and parent net income was a ¥72M loss. That looks like a broken growth story. Under the hood, the reuse segment generated ¥914M segment profit in the first nine months, up 15.8% year over year, on ¥9.92B sales. The damage came from mobile, where segment profit swung from +¥574M to -¥211M after advertising efficiency deteriorated.

The reuse data are strong enough to matter. In the Q3 presentation, Q3 standalone reuse sales rose 7.6%, gross profit rose 20.5%, and segment profit rose 27.0% to ¥395M. Personal reuse gross profit rose 21.5% and gross margin reached 43.4%, helped by better channel routing, higher purchase unit prices, and higher resale prices. The company says it receives roughly 440,000 purchase inquiries per year and is using scoring, contact center capacity, and AI-assisted workflows to route each item into the best purchase channel. That is a real operating system, not just a website.

The most underappreciated asset is Oikura. It is still small in revenue, but strategically it is becoming a low-CAC municipal disposal funnel. As of Q3, Oikura had partnerships with 317 municipalities, up 66 year over year, covering 46.7% of Japan’s population. The company says nearly 60% of Oikura’s roughly 15,000 monthly web purchase requests came from municipality flows. Q3 Oikura segment profit margin was shown at 60.7%, up from 44.5% a year earlier. If Oikura keeps scaling, it lowers customer acquisition costs for reuse supply while creating a quasi-public-sector channel that a normal marketplace would struggle to replicate.

The mobile business is the controversy. In FY2025 it produced ¥857M segment profit. In FY2026, poor ad efficiency and weaker new-line acquisition crushed margins. But the company is not saying the subscriber base is shrinking. It disclosed more than 156,000 Kashimo WiMAX lines as of March 31, 2026, and said mobile returned to single-month profitability in March, with Q4 standalone profitability still expected. Q3 mobile segment loss was only ¥24M, versus -¥50M in Q1 and -¥135M in Q2. If the business is structurally impaired, the thesis fails. If this was an acquisition-cost accident that management can fix by cutting bad spend, FY2027 earnings power is materially above the FY2026 guide.

A reasonable normalized FY2027 bridge is: reuse segment profit of ¥1.4B to ¥1.6B, mobile segment profit recovering to ¥300M to ¥600M, corporate and other costs around ¥800M to ¥900M, and consolidated operating profit of roughly ¥900M to ¥1.2B. That is not heroic. It assumes reuse continues near the current run rate and mobile regains only part of FY2025 profitability. Against a ¥7.6B EV, that is 6x to 8x normalized EBIT for a micro-cap growing revenue high single digits to low teens, with a potentially advantaged municipal reuse funnel.

The market is giving investors a strange entry point: the stock looks optically expensive because FY2026 net income guidance is almost zero, but the segment-level trough is visible. Fundabase shows the stock at ¥1,199, a ¥6.4B market cap, around 641x forecast PER, and negative recent momentum. That is a classic retail screen-out. A Japanese micro-cap with no dividend, high PBR, tiny liquidity, and a guidance cut will not attract clean multiple buyers until the consolidated numbers turn. The rerating comes if FY2027 guidance shows that mobile has stopped bleeding and reuse can carry the group.

The downside is real. This is not cash-backed. Net debt is around ¥0.75B before noncontrolling interest, and attributable book equity is only about ¥1.3B. If mobile customer acquisition is permanently worse, if WiMAX economics compress, or if reuse gross margins roll over with inventory quality, the stock can trade down hard because there is little liquidation-value support. Governance and capital return are also weak: no dividend, shareholder benefits create cost, and buybacks are not central to the story.

The upside is that very little has to go right for the stock to stop looking absurd. Q4 needs to confirm mobile profitability, Oikura needs to keep adding municipalities, and FY2027 guidance needs to show consolidated operating profit moving back toward ¥1B. If that happens, investors will stop valuing 3135 on the ¥10M FY2026 net income guide and start valuing it on normalized owner earnings from reuse plus a repaired mobile base.

Bear target: ¥650, down 46%. Mobile remains structurally loss-making, reuse growth slows, and the stock is valued as a levered low-margin retailer with negligible earnings.

Base target: ¥2,100, up 75%. FY2027 operating profit normalizes to roughly ¥1.0B to ¥1.2B, and the market values the company at about 10x EBIT, or mid-teens normalized parent earnings.

Bull target: ¥3,600, up 200%. Reuse and Oikura compound, mobile returns near FY2025 segment profitability, consolidated EBIT approaches ¥1.7B to ¥2.0B over the next cycle, and the stock rerates as a scarce listed Japanese circular-economy platform rather than a failed low-margin reseller.
